change_summary: Decider self-applied 4 patches from c6 (ISS-2200/2201/2202/2203): (1) tldr_evidence Saros/nodal conflation fix, (2) finding column Saros/nodal conflation fix, (3) detail_evidence antecedent fix, (4) tldr_verdict millennia→centuries fix. This c7 verifies all 4 patches landed cleanly and no new holes were introduced.
our_argument_summary: WIN-020 verdict is Std Model Explains. Argument: (a) the 18.613-yr lunar nodal regression is exactly what gravitational mechanics yields (post-hoc retrodiction); (b) the 18.03-yr Saros eclipse cycle — the cycle ancients actually tracked (Antikythera, Babylonian records) — differs from the nodal period by ~0.58 yr and is mathematically distinct; (c) the nodal value was formally derived from gravitational mechanics in Clairaut's mid-18th-century lunar theory; (d) the dome's 'epicyclic gears' explanation supplies no physical driver, no gear-ratio derivation, no rate, no amplitude prediction, and no testable deviation; (e) LLR confirms gravitational-torque orbit to mm precision.

relevant_files: raw-text/02-wins.txt (line 102: 'WIN-020 — Lunar Declination 18.6-Year Cycle CONFIRMED (epicyclic gears, no Newton)')
(raw-text/monitor.py absent from scrape — dome has no monitor-side handler for WIN-020)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No calculation. The dome's WIN-020 entry is a label-only confirmation — period named, gear metaphor invoked, no derivation, no observation pipeline.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Neither: no monitor.py handler exists for WIN-020. The 18.6-yr period appears only as text in the WIN list with the 'epicyclic gears, no Newton' tagline.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None — there is no monitor logic that could carry a tolerance or precondition.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. detail_verdict_text accurately states 'no monitor.py handler, no hardcoded expected value, and no live fetch' — confirmed.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Searched raw-text/ for any WIN-020 / 18.6 / nodal / saros reference in monitor.py or pull_data.py — neither file is present in the scrape, and no other file references the cycle. The 18.6-yr cycle appears only as the WIN-020 list-entry tagline (raw-text/02-wins.txt L102). No monitor-side comparison logic exists.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Standard physics: gravitational torque from the Sun on the Moon's orbital plane produces the 18.6128-yr nodal regression (Clairaut/d'Alembert/Euler mid-18th-century lunar theory; refined to mm precision by modern LLR — Williams et al., JGR Planets 1996/2006). The dome relabels this mechanism as 'epicyclic gears' without changing the numerical prediction or adding a derivation. Pure renaming.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: The numerical value (18.6 yr nodal period) is undergraduate-textbook physics, derived analytically in the 18th century and confirmed to many decimal places by LLR. The dome list-entry simply states the value as 'CONFIRMED' without timestamping evidence that the prediction preceded data acquisition. Under the dome's own 'prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled' bar, there is no monitor handler to even establish a timestamp; under the stricter trivial-transcription test (Part 3.1b), the value is freely indexed in standard references.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No derivation path from dome geometry (disc_radius=20,015 km, firmament_height=9,086 km, sun/moon altitudes) to 18.6 yr is presented anywhere — neither on the published site, nor in the WIN-020 entry, nor in any supporting page. The 'epicyclic gears' phrase is a metaphor with no gear-tooth counts, no rotational-rate calculation, no driving torque, and no amplitude prediction.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model is not wrong to reach for a mechanical metaphor here. Cyclical celestial phenomena have a long pedigree of being represented by gear-based devices — the Antikythera mechanism (cited at wins.json:WIN-020 detail_evidence via https://www.nature.com/articles/nature03397 / Freeth et al., Nature 2006) literally was an epicyclic gear computer for predicting eclipses, Saros returns, and Metonic cycles, ~100 BC. There is a genuine sense in which calling something a 'gear' captures the cyclical-and-repeating character of celestial motion (wins.json:WIN-020 detail_claim, 'lunar nodal precession (18.6-year cycle) is produced by epicyclic gears in the dome mechanism').
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The Antikythera mechanism worked because its makers reverse-engineered gear ratios from centuries of observational data — it represented observed periodicity, it did not predict it. The dome model has done none of the analogous work: no gear count, no driving torque, no rate-versus-time profile, no prediction for the major/minor lunar standstills at ~28.6°/~18.3° (wins.json:WIN-020 detail_verdict_text), no test against LLR's mm-precision observations of the gravitational-torque orbit (wins.json:WIN-020 detail_evidence, LLR sentence). A metaphor that explains nothing is decoration, not theory. Standard gravitational mechanics derives the same number from first principles and predicts amplitude, secular drift, and node-line orientation — none of which the dome's gear language touches.
kernel_of_truth.NARRATE_CITE_NOTE: Inline citations point to wins.json:WIN-020 field values pulled in this c7 run from the freshly-rebased clone (HEAD=339eae3).

deeper_analysis:
PATCH VERIFICATION — all 4 c6 patches landed cleanly:
(1) ISS-2200 / tldr_evidence: confirmed reads 'The 18.6-year lunar nodal regression was derived from gravitational mechanics by Clairaut in 1749. (Ancient astronomers tracked the related 18.03-year Saros eclipse cycle — encoded in the Antikythera mechanism — which is mathematically distinct.)' — Saros/nodal conflation in TLDR closed; Clairaut 1749 attribution to nodal; Saros explicitly named as the millennia-tracked cycle.
(2) ISS-2201 / finding: confirmed reads 'Non-discriminating: period is an input (value derived by Clairaut 1749; related Saros eclipse cycle known for >2,000 years); dome\'s gears have no physical driver or amplitude prediction' — Antikythera reference removed from finding cell; Saros/nodal split tidied; structural critique preserved.
(3) ISS-2202 / detail_evidence antecedent: confirmed second sentence now reads 'The closely related Saros eclipse cycle (~18.03 years, 223 synodic months) has been observed for over 2,000 years — encoded in the Antikythera mechanism (~100 BC) and Babylonian eclipse records — and the two cycles differ by ~0.58 years; the 18.613-year nodal value itself was formally derived from gravitational mechanics by Clairaut (1749).' — antecedent now binds 'observed for 2,000 years' to the Saros (correct) and 'Clairaut 1749' to the nodal (correct); the c5/c6 ambiguity ('This cycle has been observed for over 2,000 years') is resolved.
(4) ISS-2203 / tldr_verdict: confirmed reads 'Gravitational torque produces the exact period from first principles. The dome\'s gears have no physical driver. The value has been textbook physics for centuries (Clairaut, 1749).' — 'millennia' removed; 'centuries' (Clairaut 1749) is now arithmetically defensible (~277 yr).

ADVERSARIAL PASS — checked for new holes introduced by the patches: NONE FOUND.
- tldr_evidence (424 chars): self-consistent, faithful to detail_evidence, no new factual error. The line 'gears need a motor, and the dome doesn\'t specify one' is colloquial shorthand for 'no driving torque, no kinematic input' — a defender could pick at it (clockwork gears can be weight-driven; Antikythera was hand-cranked) but the substantive critique (no driver of any kind specified) survives the nit.
- finding (185 chars): structural critique ('non-discriminating; period is an input; dome\'s gears have no physical driver or amplitude prediction') is the right one-liner.
- detail_evidence (754 chars): citation to Nature/Freeth 2006 (https://www.nature.com/articles/nature03397) still resolves and supports the Antikythera-tracks-Saros claim. ~0.58-yr difference between Saros (18.03) and nodal (18.613): 18.6128 − 18.0299 = 0.5829 → ~0.58 ✓.
- tldr_verdict (179 chars): no new error; 'textbook physics for centuries (Clairaut, 1749)' is arithmetically and historically defensible.
- code_analysis_tags: unchanged from c6 (monitoring=none, relabels_standard=true, post_hoc=true, derives_from_dome=false). monitor.py absent from raw-text/ confirms monitoring=none.
- Verdict 'Std Model Explains' continues to match the evidence.

DEFENDER'S STRONGEST RETORT (advocate mode), now that the easy targets are patched:
(a) 'Clairaut 1749' is a specific-year attribution. Clairaut's 1749 paper to the Académie was specifically the resolution of the lunar apsidal-motion factor-of-2 problem; the full lunar theory including nodal motion was Clairaut's 1752 'Théorie de la Lune.' A meticulous historian-defender could argue we should write 'derived from gravitational mechanics in the mid-18th-century lunar theory program (Clairaut, d'Alembert, Euler, Laplace)' rather than 'Clairaut 1749.' This is hair-splitting — the substantive point (nodal regression was derived from gravity in the 18th century, ~280 yr ago, NOT millennia ago) is unaffected. The c5 and c6 reviewers both ratified the Clairaut 1749 shorthand; not re-litigating in c7. Flagging here for the record only — NOT a hole.
(b) Antikythera was actually a more complete eclipse computer than 'just the Saros' — it also tracked the Metonic cycle, the Exeligmos (3×Saros), and lunar anomalies. Our detail_evidence ('encoded in the Antikythera mechanism (~100 BC) and Babylonian eclipse records') is correct that the Saros is on the dial, but a Freeth-style nitpicker could observe that Antikythera was more than a Saros device. This does not change the argument that Antikythera tracked the SAROS (true) rather than the lunar nodal regression directly (also true — the nodal regression is implicit in the more elaborate Metonic+Saros machinery but was not a named output cycle of the device).

PERSISTENT MINOR FROM PRIOR CYCLE (NOT a c6-patch regression):
- detail_verdict_text still has mixed quote-character usage (10 straight \" + 3 curly left + 3 curly right). The curly pairs surround three hypothetical category labels ('pending-refinement,' 'preliminary-result,' 'open-for-gear-ratio-derivation'); the straight pairs surround technical jargon ('trivial-transcription test', 'predicted') and the dome-quote ('prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled'). This was c5 hole #4 (minor); c6 did not re-flag it; c6 patches did not address it. NOT a c6 regression — flagging here as a deferred minor for the decider's awareness only. Not in holes_found because c6 didn't carry it forward and re-raising would be cycle-churn.

RECOMMENDATION: no_change. The c6 patches landed cleanly; verdict holds; the four ISS-2200/2201/2202/2203 closures are confirmed substantive (not 'verify-already-done' phantom closures of the kind that bit ISS-1196 between c4 and c5).

citations_verified: https://www.nature.com/articles/nature03397 (Freeth et al., Nature 2006, 'Decoding the ancient Greek astronomical calculator known as the Antikythera Mechanism') — DOI/URL resolves; supports the Saros-tracking claim and the ~100 BC dating in detail_evidence.

Cross-ref #posthoc-standard in detail_verdict_text resolves to docs/index.html line 1497, span#posthoc-standard wrapping details#p3-31b ('3.1b Post-hoc vs Prospective — Our Standard vs the Author\'s') — anchor is live in the rendered HTML.
citations_unchecked: Clairaut (1749) — no DOI/URL provided; historical citation by name+year only. The c5/c6 reviewers accepted this as shorthand for mid-18th-century lunar theory; c7 honors that. A formal cite (e.g., Mémoires Académie Sciences 1749 / 'Du système du monde, dans les principes de la gravitation universelle') would tighten the attribution but is out of scope for a c7 verification review.

summary_for_decider: WIN-020 c6 → c7 verification: ALL FOUR c6 patches (ISS-2200/2201/2202/2203) landed cleanly. tldr_evidence/finding/detail_evidence/tldr_verdict now correctly distinguish the 18.03-yr Saros (millennia-tracked, Antikythera) from the 18.613-yr nodal regression (Clairaut 1749, mid-18th-century gravitational lunar theory). No new holes from the patches. Verdict 'Std Model Explains' holds. Recommended action: no_change. One pre-existing minor (mixed straight/curly quotes in detail_verdict_text from c5 hole #4) is still present but is NOT a c6 regression — c6 explicitly didn't carry it forward; flagging only for the record, not in holes_found, to avoid cycle-churn. Advocate-mode notes on the Clairaut 1749 attribution specificity are documented in deeper_analysis (also not a hole). Confidence 0.92 (high; patches are clean and the verdict is solid; remaining uncertainty is the persistent quote-character cosmetic and the well-trodden Clairaut shorthand).
